content: By 2025, state-of-the-art active learning agent memory systems can be repurposed to store high-value self-supervised "discovery traces" from autonomous agents, where the curiosity-driven memory pruning that prioritizes uncertain, informative data for active learning doubles as a natural filtering mechanism for self-supervised autonomous agents that need to avoid memorizing redundant, low-information environment data. This creates a self-reinforcing cycle where more useful discovery traces improve future active learning sampling, which in turn improves the quality of new self-supervised discoveries the agent makes.
